---
title: "ECS3: Instructions"
format: 
  typst:
    number-sections: true
    include-before-body:
      - text: |
          // Gör alla länkar tydliga i PDF: blå + understrukna
          #show link: set text(fill: rgb("1a5fb4"))
          #show link: underline
includesource: true
execute: 
  eval: false
---

::: callout-important
# Packages
I may use R packages below without explicitly telling you to install them first (but all packages I use are available on CRAN)!
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
# PDF or .qmd
You may read those instructions from the PDF file but please not that the source code to generate the PDF is also available in the corresponding `.qmd` file. You might prefer to work within that document!
:::

# targets?

You are recommended (although not required) to use targets for this exercise as well. If so, you may start with the following:

```{r}
# Create a template file for _targets.R which you may later modify
targets::use_targets()
# Create an empty script file where you can save your functions
usethis::use_r("functions")
```

Always make sure you don't share any sensitive data!

```{r}
usethis::edit_git_ignore("project")
```

You may instead start by a simple R script, if so initiate one in the R/folder

```{r}
usethis::use_r("draft")
```

Then, if you have time later, you may try to convert your code from that file into a more formal pipeline before submitting your final version.

No mather which option you choose, make sure to document your code by comments (including any code you might just copy from below)!
But don't just copy my code! I may use some fairly compact data.table syntax for example. If you don't want to write data.table syntax yourself, you should instead interpret my code but implement the same thing using tools which you are more comfortable with (for example dplyr/tidyverse). The same goes for my use of parquet files and duckplyr. Use that if you like (then you need to add comments to your code to demonstrate that you understand what it does), otherwise use any other tool you prefer (as long as the code can be re-run later).

::: callout-note
## git

Don't forget to regularly commit your changes, both locally and to GitHub!
:::

# Patient data

We need some patient data:

```{r}
if (!fs::file_exists("data.zip")) {
  curl::curl_download(
    "https://github.com/eribul/cs/raw/refs/heads/main/data.zip",
    "data.zip",
    quiet = FALSE
  )
}
```

We used `data.table::fread()` in the previous session, which is a very fast and competent reader for CSV files, but it can not read data directly from a ZIP archive. To avoid unzipping the whole file (we do not need it right now), we can use `read_csv()` instead:

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(data.table)
patients <-
  readr::read_csv(unz("data.zip", "data-fixed/patients.csv")) |>
  setDT() |>
  setkey(id)
```

Sometimes there might be whole columns or rows missing in a dataset. We won't have much use for those so let's remove them if so

```{r}
patients <- janitor::remove_empty(patients, quiet = FALSE)
# A column with only one constant value is also not very interesting
patients <- janitor::remove_constant(patients, quiet = FALSE)
```

# Expectations/validations

What are our expectations of the data? Are those expectations full-filled or do we need some additional modifications or perhaps deletion of some data? Check out the [pointblank](https://rstudio.github.io/pointblank/articles/VALID-I.html) package!

```{r}
library(pointblank)
```

I'll give you some of my expectations below but there should probably be more! What do you expect for `marital`? Would "kulbo" be a valid value here? Is everyone supposed to be either male or female? Can we have data for individuals born in the 17th centuary?

1.  Replace all my labels with something more informative below
2.  Try to add 3 more expectations with informative labels

```{r}
checks <-
  patients |>
  create_agent(label = "A very simple example.") |>
  col_vals_between(
    where(is.Date),
    as.Date("1900-01-01"),
    as.Date(Sys.Date()),
    na_pass = TRUE,
    label = "WHAT DO WE CHECK HERE? DESCRIBE!"
  ) |>
  col_vals_gte(
    deathdate,
    vars(birthdate),
    na_pass = TRUE,
    label = "ADD SOMETHING!"
  ) |>
  col_vals_regex(
    ssn, # This variable was described in ECS1!
    "[0-9]{3}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{4}$",
    label = "WHAT ON EARTH DOES THIS MEAN?"
  ) |>
  col_is_integer(
    id,
    label = "ADD ..."
  ) |>
  interrogate()

checks
```

When you are happy with your validations, export your report using:

```{r}
export_report(checks, "patient_validation.html")
```

Then commit your changes to your Git repo with an informative message (don't change the filename from `patient_validation.html`!). I will check later that you have submited a file with that name!

## Factors?

Some columns have just abbreviations for their values. Let's convert those to something more informative. In this case we don't actually know what the different marital status are but we might have an educated guess (or was there any information from the codebook used in ECS2?).

::: callout-warning
## Ask for help

To just guess is obviously dangerous in a real world scenario! Lacking documentation, however, is a very real problem in many cases! But always try to confirm your guesses with someone and carefully document what you don't know!
:::

```{r}
patients[, .N, marital] # Run it!
```

My best guess would be:

```{r}
patients[,
  marital := factor(
    marital,
    levels = c("S", "M", "D", "W"),
    labels = c("Single", "Married", "Divorced", "Widowed")
  )
]
```

Add factor levels for all variables deemed relevant. Use your code_scans from ECS1 or perhaps something like this (if using this code you need to understand what it does):

```{r}
fctr_candidates <-
  patients[, which(lapply(.SD, uniqueN) < 10), .SDcols = is.character] |>
  names()

patients[,
  lapply(.SD, \(x) paste(unique(x), collapse = ", ")),
  .SDcols = fctr_candidates
] |>
  glimpse()
```

Some variables might also look like good factor candidates but might already have good enough labels. Just convert those to factors by something like:

```{r}
#| eval: false
patients[,
  names(.SD) := lapply(as.factor),
  .SDcols = c("____", "____", ..., "____")
]
```

## Unusual combinations?

How many individuals are there in the patients data set? It might be enough for statistical analyses but we might also consider disclosure control. What happens for example if we tabulate by gender, race and state?

```{r}
patients[, .N, .(gender, race, state)][order(N)]
```

Whould it be sensible to present data in a later stage where individual persons might be able to identify themselves? Do you have the ethical permission to present such data (you obviously had the permission to retrieve the data, but that is not the same thing)? Should certain groups be combined when analyzed? Maybe we don't need such detailed classification of "race" for example? Use `forcats::fct_lump_prop()` to reclassify that variable.

# Derived variables

How old are these people? We know when they were born, so let's subtract that from todays date and calculate the whole number of times they have circled the sun so far

```{r}
patients[, age := as.integer((as.IDate(Sys.Date()) - birthdate)) %/% 365.241]
patients[, hist(age)]
```

Did we forget something? Maybe we should focus on the living?

```{r}
patients[is.na(deathdate), hist(age)]
```

Hmm, living by the time the data was collected ... but now we look at their age TODAY. Is this right? When was the original data set generated? Let's say we don't now ... (Yes, you should know if this was your own data, but reality is not always perfect). We have data for `payer_transitions` as well. Since this is US data, such things are important to their health care. We might check when the last transaction was performed and might guess that the data snapshot was taken soon after that.

We only need a single date however, so no need to read the whole file (no problem with this toy data but you might have massive data sets later in life). First, unzip only the relevant file and then use duckdb to perform some calculations on disk.

```{r}
unzip("data.zip", files = "data-fixed/payer_transitions.csv")
lastdate <-
  duckplyr::read_csv_duckdb("data-fixed/payer_transitions.csv") |>
  summarise(lastdate = max(end_date)) |>
  collect() |>
  pluck("lastdate") |>
  as.Date()
```

What date was it? Is it reasonable? Well, probably not! We were not careful enough when chosing the column. The `end_date` might be in the future for enrolled payers! Use the `start_date` instead!

Now make a histogram of the living patients age distribution at the time of the assumed data extract (we have no idea what happend thereafter)!

# Names

::: callout-note
To have access to real patients names might seem unrealistic but let's assume you need it to prepare individualised letters to ask them to fill in an additional questionaire. 
:::

How should we address those people?

```{r}
patients[,
  names(.SD) := lapply(.SD, \(x) replace_na(x, "")),
  .SDcols = c("prefix", "middle")
]
patients[,
  full_name := paste(
    prefix,
    first,
    middle,
    last,
    fifelse(!suffix %in% c("", NA), paste0(", ", suffix), "")
  )
]
patients[, full_name]
```

Hm, some people got trailing white spaces (can you see why this happened?) Lets remove those and let's in fact do that for all character variables:

```{r}
patients[, names(.SD) := lapply(.SD, trimws), .SDcols = is.character]
```

Actually we have some duplicated spaces between names as well. Lets remove them by a simple regular expression:

```{r}
patients[, full_name := stringr::str_replace(full_name, "  ", " ")]
```

With this new name variable, we might no longer need some of the original columns:

```{r}
patients[, c("prefix", "first", "middle", "last", "suffix", "maiden") := NULL]
```

# Necessary data?

Do we have any use of the `drivers` column? Mayby if we want to link the data to additional sources. Could be interesting for example to see for how long an individual had his/her license before being involved in a traffic accident. We don't have that info now so the only relevant information might be if an individual has a license or not.

```{r}
patients[, driver := !is.na(drivers)][, drivers := NULL]
```

The same probably goes for several other columns. We could as well delete them (to save space and therefore computational time etc; however this only applies to the intermediate data sets, don't delete the source data!).

The lat and long columns are probably most relevant if we want to calculate distances to other places. Does individuals with a longer/shorter distance to their nearest hospital recieve better/worse care? Is the incidence of skin cancer more prevalent if you live closer to the sea?

But it might also just be fun to play with visualisations some times. This might also be useful to decide how representative the sample is.

```{r}
leaflet::leaflet(data = patients) |>
  leaflet::addTiles() |>
  leaflet::addMarkers(~lon, ~lat, label = ~full_name)
```

Based on this data, would it be reasonable to make statistical inference for the whole of USA? For the whole world?

# Linkage

The patients data might not be very interesting on its own, if we are interested in
anything of clinical/medical relevance.
Let's say for example that we are interested in reasons for procedures performed on adults over the years.
First we need to define adult, lets say individuals of at least 18 years of age. Age when?
At the time of their procedure seems reasonable. Such data, however, is not part of the
`patients` table so we first need to join/link it to the `procedures` table.
But let's convert all data sets to parquet files first ... just for fun :-)

```{r}
zip::unzip("data.zip")
fs::dir_create("data-parquet")
csv2parquet <- function(file) {
  new_file <-
    file |>
    stringr::str_replace("-fixed", "-parquet") |>
    stringr::str_replace(".csv", ".parquet")

  duckplyr::read_csv_duckdb(file) |>
    duckplyr::compute_parquet(new_file)
}
fs::dir_ls("data-fixed/") |>
  purrr::walk(csv2parquet, .progress = TRUE)
fs::dir_delete("data-fixed")
```

So we needed the procedure data this time:

```{r}
procedures <- duckplyr::read_parquet_duckdb("data-parquet/procedures.parquet")
```

Let's just load the data we actually need (inspect the data and use all information available from ECS1 such as code_scans and codebook etc). 

- We need the patient id for linkage (`patient`)
- We need the resons for their procedure (variable `reasoncode_icd10`, which was actuelly not in the codebook)
- we need a date to calculate their age and to summarise patterns over the years

Actually, we might only be interested in observations where we actually have a `reasoncode_icd10`

```{r}
procedures <-
  procedures |>
  select(patient, reasoncode_icd10, start) |>
  filter(!is.na(reasoncode_icd10)) |>
  collect()
```

Now, when we have the data in memory, let's use data.table instead

```{r}
setDT(procedures, key = "patient")
```

We only need the year of the procedure (not the exact time)

```{r}
procedures[, year := year(start)][, start := NULL]
```

So lets join the necessary patient data with this new dataset, filter out the adults and tabulate their resons for their performed procedures over the years:


```{r}
proc_n_adults <-
  procedures[
    patients[, .(id, birthdate = as.IDate(birthdate))],
    on = c(patient = "id")
  ] |>
  _[year - year(birthdate) >= 18L, .N, .(reasoncode_icd10, year)]
```

Note that the same patient might have had more than one procedure etc!

The `reasoncode_icd10` is just a code, probably not to interesting for a human reader.
If there is a code, however, we might also add a more verbose description using a reference tabel included in the `{decoder}` package.

```{r}
cond_by_year <- setDT(decoder::icd10se)[
  proc_n_adults,
  on = c(key = "reasoncode_icd10")
]
```

Hmm, the description is in Swedish while English would be prefered ... well you might use the example code provided for JSON data and ICD-10 codes as included in the end of EL5. You can try on your own if you like! Otherwise use your favorite translation tool for now to better understand your results.

Lets make a visualisation for the five most common conditions overall:

```{r}
top5 <- cond_by_year[, .(N = sum(N)), .(value)][order(-N)][1:5, value]
ggplot(cond_by_year[.(top5), on = "value"], aes(year, N, color = value)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  guides(color = guide_legend(ncol = 1)) +
  scale_color_discrete(
    labels = function(x) str_wrap(x, width = 40)
  )
```

- Wath happens in the end? Is it really reasonable to include tha last year (we were looking at the assumed data extraction date earlier)
- What happened early in history? Do we actually have all relevant data already from the start or should we focus on years which are more acurately recorded?
- Does this visualisation tell us anything? Are certain conditions more common today or do we need to standardize the numbers with account to population size or health seeking behaviour etc.
- Are patients sicker today or do they get more treatment for conditions which might have been undertreated in the past?

# Submit

Don't forget to commit and push all of your code to GitHub. Then, indicate in canvas that you are done!
I will look at your codes and comments, either in the `_targets.R` file or in the `R/draft.R` file.
